title: Mean Squared Error (MSE)
concept: Mean Squared Error (MSE) squares each prediction error and averages them, penalizing large mistakes much more heavily than small ones.

exercises.prompt: Recap: Why can't we just add up raw positive and negative errors to find the total cost score?

options.text: Positive and negative errors would cancel each other out
The computer would run out of memory
Negative numbers do not exist in math

exercises.explain: Right — raw addition allows positive and negative errors to cancel out, making a poor model look perfect.

cards.title: Grading rules
cards.body: Imagine a teacher grading exams. Missing a tiny detail loses you 1 point. But missing an entire section loses you 10 points. The penalty grows faster than the error.

cards.title: What is Mean Squared Error?
cards.body: Mean Squared Error (MSE) is the standard cost function for regression. It takes each prediction error (residual), squares it, and averages the results.

cards.title: Clean-up duties
cards.body: Squaring errors is like leaving clothes on the floor. Leaving one sock is fine. Leaving ten isn't ten times worse — it's a full-blown disaster!

cards.title: How squaring grows
cards.body: If you are off by 2 units, the squared penalty is 4. If you are off by 10, the squared penalty is 100! The penalty became 25 times larger for an error only 5 times bigger.

cards.title: Not just for positive numbers
cards.body: While squaring does remove negative signs, its main purpose is to penalize large errors heavily. It forces the model to fix huge misses first.

cards.title: Linear vs. squared error
cards.body: How the penalty scales for an error of 10:

exercises.prompt: How does MSE handle a prediction error of 4 units?

options.text: It squares the error, resulting in a penalty of 16
It takes the square root, resulting in a penalty of 2
It doubles the error, resulting in a penalty of 8

exercises.explain: MSE squares each error (residual). Since 4 squared is 16, the penalty is 16.

exercises.prompt: True or false: MSE penalizes large errors much more severely than small errors.
exercises.explain: True! Because it squares the errors, larger errors create disproportionately higher penalties.
Mean Squared Error squares the residuals and averages them.

exercises.prompt: For errors of 1, 2, and 3, what is the calculated Mean Squared Error (MSE)?
exercises.explain: The squared errors are 1, 4, and 9. Their sum is 14. The mean is 14 divided by 3, which is approximately 4.67.

exercises.prompt: You are predicting flight delays. A 5-minute delay is minor, but a 120-minute delay ruins a traveler's day completely. Why is MSE a good cost function here?

options.text: It penalizes the 120-minute delay exponentially more, forcing the model to avoid huge delays
It ignores the 120-minute delay to keep the flight schedule clean
It automatically adjusts all flight delays to be exactly 10 minutes

exercises.explain: Outstanding! MSE's squared penalty forces the model to focus heavily on preventing large errors. Next: what if we don't want to penalize outliers so harshly?
